description: A storyteller brings a tale into being. Neighbours may do the same with a ceremony, or designers with a new arrangement. 'kealo' centres the arrival of a work, idea, or organised possibility that did not previously exist in that form; 'pilewa' (make) follows the work that produces a result, and 'lorima' (build) assembles parts into a structure.
articulatory_notes: Press the back of the tongue against the soft palate for unvoiced 'k' and release into middle-height front unrounded 'e'. Keep the following stressed open central 'a' in a separate syllable, then touch the tongue tip to the alveolar ridge for 'l' and finish with middle-height back rounded 'o'.
sound_symbolism: The clear hiatus between 'e' and stressed 'a' opens the middle of 'kealo'. The word seems to make room before closing with rounded 'lo'.
usage_notes: The created work, story, idea, or arrangement is the direct object. The verb does not imply creation from nothing; makers can still name their materials, influences, collaborators, and conditions.

semantic_domains.activity: bringing a new work, idea, or arrangement into existence
semantic_domains.creation: the emergence of something not previously present in that form
